radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  ___ man with persistent left lung collapse.

Comparison is made to the chest radiograph of ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Axial MDCT images of the chest were obtained after administration
of 75 mL of Optiray intravenously.  Sagittal and coronal reformatted images
were then acquired.

FINDINGS:  8 cm mass within the left lower lobe grows into the left lower lobe
bronchus and is associated with complete collapse of the left lower lobe.  The
post-radiation changes of the left upper lobe and superior segment of the
right lower lobe are noted. Mild atelectasis of the right lung base is noted
and the remainder of the right lung is clear. The left pleural effusion is
small.  

Partial calcification of the mural thrombus in the left main pulmonary artery
indicates it is chronic.  Aside from an one 11 mm prevascular node, no central
nodes are pathologically enlarged.  The endotracheal tube and NG tube are in
the standard positions. Small amount of pericardial effusion with no evidence
of tamponade. 22 x 31 mm right thyroid lobe and a calcified 13 x 12 mm left
thyroid nodule.

There is a 22 x 20 mm left adrenal mass, which cannot be further characterized
on this study.  There is also a 7-mm enhancing lesion within the right lobe of
the liver.  Calcified granulomas of the liver are noted.  The gallbladder,
spleen, left adrenal gland and the superior pole of the kidneys appear
unremarkable.

BONE WINDOWS:  No concerning lytic or sclerotic lesions are identified.
Left humeral fracture.

IMPRESSION:

1. 8 cm mass of the left lower lobe grows into the left main stem bronchus and
is associated with complete collapse of the left lower lobe. Post- radiation
changes of the left lung and the superior segment of right lung are noted.  

2.  Chronic, nonoccluding embolism within the left main pulmonary artery.

3.  Right adrenal mass, which cannot be further evaluated on this study.
Differential diagnosis includes metastasis  and adenoma. If further evaluation
is required, MRI of the abdomen or CT of the adrenals can be obtained.

4.  7-mm enhancing lesion of the right lobe of the liver.  Differential
diagnoses include flash-filling hemangioma and FNH.

5. Fracture of the proximal left humerus.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CTA CHEST WITH CONTRAST

INDICATION:  History: ___ with cardiac arrest// eval for pE

TECHNIQUE:  Axial multidetector CT images were obtained through the thorax
after the uneventful administration of intravenous contrast.  Reformatted
coronal, sagittal, thin slice axial images, and oblique maximal intensity
projection images were submitted to PACS and reviewed.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 3.0 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 9.7 mGy (Body) DLP = 4.9
mGy-cm.
   2) Spiral Acquisition 4.5 s, 35.3 cm; CTDIvol = 6.3 mGy (Body) DLP = 222.1
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 227 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  None

FINDINGS: 

HEART AND VASCULATURE: Pulmonary vasculature is well opacified to the
subsegmental level without filling defect to indicate a pulmonary embolus. The
thoracic aorta is normal in caliber without evidence of dissection or
intramural hematoma.  Within the descending aorta superior to the celiac
access are hypodensities within the lumen, which be mixing of contrast rather
than intraluminal thrombus (3; 17).  There is reflux of contrast into the IVC.
The heart, pericardium, and great vessels are within normal limits. No
pericardial effusion is seen.

AXILLA, HILA, AND MEDIASTINUM: No axillary, mediastinal, or hilar
lymphadenopathy is present.  No mediastinal mass.

PLEURAL SPACES: No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

LUNGS/AIRWAYS: Ground-glass opacities and septal thickening right greater than
left suggest possible mild pulmonary edema.  0.5 cm pulmonary nodule in the
right lower lobe (3; 54) is noted.  The airways are patent to the level of the
segmental bronchi bilaterally.

BASE OF NECK: Hypodense thyroid nodule in the thyroid gland measuring 1.2 x
2.3 cm is noted.  ET tube is partially visualized in terminates above the
carina.

ABDOMEN:  Included portion of the upper abdomen is unremarkable.  Enteric tube
is partially visualized within the stomach.

BONES: No suspicious osseous abnormality is seen.? mild deformity of the upper
sternal body may be secondary to prior resuscitation efforts.

IMPRESSION:


1. No evidence of pulmonary embolism or aortic abnormality.
2. Hypodensity within the descending aorta superior to the celiac axis may
represent mixing of contrast and artifact rather than intraluminal thrombus.
3. 2.3 cm hypodense thyroid lesion, correlate with prior thyroid ultrasound.
4. Mild deformity of the proximal sternum may be secondary to resuscitation
efforts.
5. Bilateral ground-glass opacities and septal thickening may represent mild
pulmonary edema.
6. Reflux of contrast into the IVC suggests a component of cardiac
dysfunction.

NOTIFICATION:   The findings were discussed with Dr. ___. by ___
___, M.D. on the telephone on ___ at 6:26 am, 5 minutes after discovery
of the findings.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/O CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with prior abd CT showing 3 nodular pulmonary
densities in the left basilar region measuring up to 8 x 8 mm. These findings
may may represent areas of rounded atelectasis, however short-term followup
with nonemergent CT chest is recommended.  // f/u pulmonary nodules

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector helical scanning of the chest was performed without
intravenous contrast agent reconstructed as contiguous 5- and 1.25-mm thick
axial, 2.5-mm thick coronal and parasagittal, and 8 x 8 mm MIPs axial images.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 4.3 s, 33.5 cm; CTDIvol = 23.2 mGy (Body) DLP = 777.7
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 778 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  ___

FINDINGS: 

The thyroid is normal. Supraclavicular, axillary, mediastinal and hilar lymph
nodes are not enlarged. Aorta and pulmonary arteries are normal size. Cardiac
configuration is normal.  Patient is status post CABG, there appears to be
also coronary stents.  There is no pleural or pericardial effusion
There are scattered tiny calcified granulomas.  There is minimal paraseptal
emphysema.
There is a 3 mm right perifissural nodule (4:106) represents an intrapulmonary
lymph node
3 mm subpleural nodule right lower lobe is stable (4:113)
There are few subpleural micro nodules (4:31, 63, 72, 81, 95, 128),
statistically benign
Subpleural minimal nodularity in the inferior aspect of the right middle lobe
(4:146) is stable.
Subpleural opacities in the left lower lobe are stable from ___
abdomen CT, not evaluated in ___ due to the presence of left pleural
effusion.
This examination is not tailored for subdiaphragmatic evaluation, there is
fatty infiltration of the liver.  There are multiple stones within the
gallbladder

IMPRESSION: 

Subpleural opacities in the left lower lobe stable from ___ correspond to
rounded atelectasis
Micronodules, are statistically benign no require follow-up
Emphysema
Fatty liver
Cholelithiasis

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  Chest CT with contrast

INDICATION:  ___ with new cerebellar mass.  Right for thoracic metastases.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axial images were acquired through the chest, abdomen and
pelvis following intravenous contrast administration with split bolus
technique.
IV Contrast:  130 mL Omnipaque.
Oral contrast was not administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 4.9 s, 65.2 cm; CTDIvol = 5.6 mGy (Body) DLP = 366.4
mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 0.6 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 3.0 mGy (Body) DLP = 1.5
mGy-cm.
   3) Stationary Acquisition 4.8 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 24.4 mGy (Body) DLP =
12.2 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 380 mGy-cm.
** Note: This radiation dose report was copied from CLIP ___ (CT ABD AND
PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

COMPARISON:  None.

FINDINGS: 

NECK, THORACIC INLET, AXILLAE, CHEST WALL: Thyroid is unremarkable without
nodules warranting further evaluation.  No supraclavicular or axillary
lymphadenopathy.  Largest axillary node on the right measures 7 mm (02:20). 
No suspicious lesions in the chest wall.

UPPER ABDOMEN: Please refer to separate report for abdominopelvic CT from the
same day for detailed abdominopelvic findings.

MEDIASTINUM: No mass or lymphadenopathy.  Several lower left paratracheal
nodes are not pathologically enlarged, measuring up 8 mm (302:78).  Subcarinal
node is in the upper limits of normal measuring approximately 10 mm (302:106).

HILA: No hilar mass or lymphadenopathy.

HEART and PERICARDIUM: Heart is normal size.  The aorta is tortuous and mildly
calcified.  Moderate calcifications are seen in coronary arteries, mild in the
aortic valve.  No pericardial effusion.
PLEURA: No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.
LUNG:

1. PARENCHYMA:  Extensive upper lobe dominant centrilobular emphysema.  Left
paramedian spiculated mass abutting the aortic arch measures 2.2 x 1.7 cm
(302: 47).  Also noted is a right upper lobe nodule 1.3 cm with associated
peribronchial thickening and bronchiectasis (302:25).
2. AIRWAYS: Traction bronchiectasis most notable in the right upper lobe. 
Bilateral scattered endobronchial mucous plugging, more extensive at the
segmental level in the lower lobes.
3. VESSELS: Pulmonary artery is normal caliber.  No central pulmonary emboli.
CHEST CAGE: No suspicious osseous lesions or acute fracture.

IMPRESSION:


1. Left paramedian spiculated mass measuring up to 2.2 cm.  Amenable to
endobronchial tissue sampling.
2. Irregular right upper lobe nodule, also suspicious for malignancy.
3. Extensive centrilobular emphysema with bronchiectasis and scattered mucous
plugging.

2. AIRWAYS: Traction bronchiectasis most notable in the right upper lobe. 
Bilateral scattered endobronchial mucous plugging, more extensive at the
segmental level in the lower lobes.
3. VESSELS: Pulmonary artery is normal caliber.  No central pulmonary emboli.
CHEST CAGE: No suspicious osseous lesions or acute fracture.
sections.impression:
1. Left paramedian spiculated mass measuring up to 2.2 cm.  Amenable to
endobronchial tissue sampling.
2. Irregular right upper lobe nodule, also suspicious for malignancy.
3. Extensive centrilobular emphysema with bronchiectasis and scattered mucous
plugging.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CTA CHEST WITH CONTRAST

INDICATION:  History: ___ with dyspnea and tachycardia// eval for pe

TECHNIQUE:  Axial multidetector CT images were obtained through the thorax
after the uneventful administration of intravenous contrast.  Reformatted
coronal, sagittal, thin slice axial images, and oblique maximal intensity
projection images were submitted to PACS and reviewed.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 0.5 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 2.4 mGy (Body) DLP = 1.2
mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 4.5 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 21.7 mGy (Body) DLP =
10.8 mGy-cm.
   3) Spiral Acquisition 3.4 s, 27.1 cm; CTDIvol = 26.9 mGy (Body) DLP = 729.0
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 741 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  CT torso dated ___.

FINDINGS: 

HEART AND VASCULATURE: There are pulmonary emboli extending from the distal
right main pulmonary artery to lobar to segmental pulmonary arteries in the
right upper and middle lobes and to lobar to subsegmental pulmonary arteries
in the right lower lobe.  The main pulmonary artery and left pulmonary
arteries and its branches are patent without filling defect.  No evidence of
right heart strain.  Some atherosclerotic changes are seen along the aorta, at
the aortic arch.  are mild to moderate coronary artery calcifications.  No
pericardial effusion.

AXILLA, HILA, AND MEDIASTINUM: No axillary, mediastinal, or hilar
lymphadenopathy is present.  No mediastinal mass.

PLEURAL SPACES: No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

LUNGS/AIRWAYS: There is bilateral lower lung linear subsegmental atelectasis. 
Scattered areas of subsegmental atelectasis seen elsewhere, including in the
bilateral upper lobes, right middle lobe, and lingula.  No focal
consolidation.  An azygos lobe is incidentally noted.

BASE OF NECK: Scattered hypoattenuating nodule in the right thyroid lobe are
noted measuring up to asthma in 7 mm.

ABDOMEN: Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen demonstrate slight nodular
contour of the liver parenchyma.  The spleen may be surgically absent.

BONES: No suspicious osseous abnormality is seen.? There is no acute fracture.
Degenerative changes of thoracic spine are moderate.  Evidence of DISH is seen
along the thoracic spine.

IMPRESSION: 

1.  Pulmonary emboli extending from the distal right main pulmonary artery to
segmental level in right upper and middle lobes and subsegmental level in
right lower lobe.  No left-sided pulmonary emboli.  Difficult to exclude right
heart strain.  Echocardiogram would further assess.
2.  No focal consolidation.
3.  Mild nodular contour of the liver raise concern for cirrhosis. 
Correlation with liver function test is recommended for further evaluation.
4.  Status post splenectomy.

NOTIFICATION:  The findings were discussed with ___, M.D. by
___, M.D. on the telephone on ___ at 4:53 pm, 2 minutes after
discovery of the findings.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CTA CHEST AND CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS

INDICATION:  History: ___ with fever, tachycardia, clear chest x-ray, hx
ulcerative colitis  // PE, pneumonia, intraabdominal abscess?

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axial images were acquired through the chest, abdomen and
pelvis following intravenous contrast administration with split bolus
technique.
Oral contrast was not administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 1.0 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 3.0 mGy (Body) DLP = 1.5
mGy-cm.
   2) Spiral Acquisition 2.9 s, 22.6 cm; CTDIvol = 14.0 mGy (Body) DLP = 315.8
mGy-cm.
   3) Spiral Acquisition 7.3 s, 57.1 cm; CTDIvol = 16.1 mGy (Body) DLP = 920.1
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 1,237 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  MRI abdomen dated ___.  CT abdomen dated ___.

FINDINGS: 

CHEST:

HEART AND VASCULATURE: The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber without
evidence of acute injury.  No pericardial effusion is seen.

AXILLA, HILA, AND MEDIASTINUM: No axillary, mediastinal, or hilar
lymphadenopathy is present.  No mediastinal mass or hematoma.

PLEURAL SPACES: No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

LUNGS/AIRWAYS: The airways are patent to the level of the segmental bronchi
bilaterally.  There are bibasilar atelectasis.  There is a 1.4 cm focal
opacification in the left upper lobe (series 4, image 71) which may represent
early pneumonia.


ABDOMEN:

HEPATOBILIARY: The liver demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout. 
There is no evidence of focal lesion or laceration.  There is no evidence of
intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary dilatation.  The gallbladder is not
visualized.

PANCREAS: The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions or pancreatic ductal dilatation.  There is no peripancreatic
stranding.

SPLEEN: The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesion or laceration.

ADRENALS: The right adrenal gland is surgically removed.  The left adrenal
gland is normal.

URINARY: The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with normal nephrogram. 
There is no evidence of focal renal lesions or hydronephrosis.  There is no
perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL: The stomach is unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate
normal caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement throughout.  There is moderate
amount of stool throughout the colon and rectum.  There is equivocal mild
thickening of the distal sigmoid colon/rectosigmoid.

There is no free fluid or free air in the abdomen.

PELVIS:

There is air visualized in the urinary bladder likely secondary to recent
instrumentation.  There is a Foley catheter visualized with the balloon at the
base of the penis.  There is no free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS: The reproductive organs are unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES: There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR: There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm or retroperitoneal hematoma. 
No atherosclerotic disease is noted.

BONES: There is mild degenerative changes of the thoracolumbar spine.  There
is left hip posterior dislocation which is likely chronic with adjacent soft
tissue thickening, adjacent joint effusion not excluded..  There is chronic
deformity of the left femoral head and left femoral head/hip degenerative
changes..  There is no evidence of bone erosion.

SOFT TISSUES: The abdominal and pelvic wall is within normal limits.  There is
a pacer versus stimulator hardware visualized in the right lower quadrant
subcutaneous soft tissue.

IMPRESSION:


1. Nonspecific 1.4 cm nodular left upper lobe opacity which may represent
pneumonia.  Recommend follow-up CT chest in 3 months to assess for resolution.
Pulmonary nodule not excluded.
2. Malpositioned Foley catheter with balloon in the base of the penis.
3. Moderate amount stool in the distal sigmoid colon/rectosigmoid.  Equivocal
associated mild wall thickening, possible early stercoral colitis.
4. Chronic appearing left hip dislocation with adjacent soft tissue
thickening, adjacent joint effusion not excluded..

NOTIFICATION:  The findings of the study had been discussed a by Dr. ___
with Dr. ___ on ___ at 11:54 p.m.

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  Near syncope and elevated D-dimer.  Assess for PE.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT-acquired axial images were obtained through the lungs in a
low-dose non-contrast fashion.  Subsequent attempt to perform CT angiogram was
unsuccessful due to insufficient IV access.  Attempt was made to recall the
patient; however, the patient was discharged against medical advice.

COMPARISONS:  None available.

FINDINGS:  There is no axillary, supraclavicular or hilar adenopathy.  A 10-mm
subcarinal node is noted.  The esophagus is normal.  Heart and pericardium are
normal, without pericardial effusion.  

The trachea and central airways are patent to the segmental level.  Extensive
paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema is noted.  A 4-mm right lower lobe
nodule is seen (2:40).  There is no pleural effusion or pneumothorax. 
Assessment for PE is not possible in the absence of intravenous contrast.  

Though this study is not tailored for subdiaphragmatic evaluation, limited
view of the upper abdomen demonstrates multiple hepatic hypodensities, several
of which are too small to be fully characterized, the largest three of which
may be simple cysts.  Slight nodular contour to the liver suggests cirrhosis.
Gallbladder is surgically absent.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:  There is no lytic or sclerotic bone lesion concerning for
osseous malignant process.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Limited study, both due to the absence of intravenous contrast as well as
a low-dose technique as described above.
2.  Moderate-to-severe emphysema with 4-mm right lower lobe nodule, for which
followup evaluation in one year's time is recommended.
3.  Hepatic hypodensities, which are too small to be characterized, some of
which are simple cysts.

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  ___ with with lung cancer on chemo p/w weakness and dyspnea, had
desaturdation to 85% on EMS arrivalNO_PO contrast  // eval for PE vs
dissection

TECHNIQUE:  Axial multidetector CT images were obtained through the thorax
after the uneventful administration of intravenous contrast in the arterial
phase.  Then, imaging was obtained through the abdomen and pelvis in the
portal venous phase. Reformatted coronal and sagittal images through the
chest, abdomen, and pelvis, and oblique maximal intensity projection images of
the chest were submitted to PACS and reviewed.

DOSE:  Total DLP (Body) = 680 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  CT chest ___
CT abdomen pelvis ___

FINDINGS: 

CHEST:

HEART AND VASCULATURE: Pulmonary vasculature is well opacified to the
subsegmental level without filling defect to indicate a pulmonary embolus. 
The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber without evidence of dissection or
intramural hematoma.  The heart, pericardium, and great vessels are within
normal limits. No pericardial effusion is seen.

AXILLA, HILA, AND MEDIASTINUM:  Bilateral prominent axillary lymph nodes are
unchanged in size compared to prior CT.  There is no mediastinal hilar
lymphadenopathy is present.  No mediastinal mass.

PLEURAL SPACES: No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

LUNGS/AIRWAYS:  A partially cavitated, heterogeneous and poorly enhancing
spiculated left upper lobe lesion is similar in size to CT from ___ measuring approximately 2.2 x 3.1 cm, however measuring 1.7 x 2.0 cm in
___.  There is centrilobular emphysema.   The airways are patent to the
level of the segmental bronchi bilaterally.

BASE OF NECK: Visualized portions of the base of the neck show no abnormality.

ABDOMEN:

HEPATOBILIARY: The liver demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout. 
There is no evidence of focal lesions.  There is no evidence of intrahepatic
or extrahepatic biliary dilatation.  The gallbladder is within normal limits.

PANCREAS: The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions or pancreatic ductal dilatation. There is no peripancreatic
stranding.

SPLEEN: The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS: Since prior CT in ___ there are unchanged bilateral
heterogeneously hypoenhancing adrenal metastasis measuring 6.2 x 5.3 cm on the
right and 5.5 x 5.5 cm on the left (previously measuring 2.5 x 1.8 cm and 2.4
x 1.7 cm, respectively, in ___.  The right adrenal mass has mild
mass effect on the IVC which remains patent (2b:135).

URINARY: Several well-circumscribed hypodensities in bilateral renal cortices
are compatible with simple renal cysts.  Smaller hypodensities elsewhere in
the renal cortices are too small to characterize but likely represent renal
cyst.  Otherwise, the kidneys enhance normally and symmetrically.  There is no
evidence of hydronephrosis. There is no perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL: The stomach is unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate
normal caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement throughout.  Diffuse
diverticulosis of the colon, particularly the sigmoid colon is noted, without
evidence of wall thickening and fat stranding.  The appendix is normal.  There
is no free intraperitoneal fluid or free air.

PELVIS: The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no
free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS: The prostate and seminal vesicles are unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES: There is more conspicuous retroperitoneal lymph nodes compared to
priors with the, including at least 3 centrally necrotic lymph nodes noted the
located between the aorta and pancreas (2b:143, 139, 138).  No mesenteric
lymphadenopathy.  There is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR: There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Moderate atherosclerotic
disease is noted.

BONES AND SOFT TISSUES:  A left soft tissue nodule deep to the left gluteus
musculature is slightly enlarged compared to prior CT, now measuring 1.3 x 1.0
cm, previously measuring 1.0 x 0.8 cm.  The bony structures are essentially
unchanged from prior CT, including healing pathologic left ninth, tenth and
right eighth rib fractures.

IMPRESSION:


1. No evidence of acute pulmonary embolism or aortic abnormality.
2. Interval worsening and enlargement of retroperitoneal lymph nodes,
specifically with development of at least 3 centrally necrotic lymph nodes
along the posterior aspect of the pancreas.
3. Slight interval increase in size of left gluteal soft tissue nodule since ___.
4. Bilateral adrenal metastatic lesions are unchanged in size from ___ but significantly larger than ___.
5. Unchanged left upper lobe pulmonary mass.

radiology_facts.report_text:
REASON FOR EXAMINATION:  Followup of pulmonary nodule.

COMPARISON:  Chest CT from ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Unenhanced MDCT of the chest was obtained from the level of
thoracic inlet to the level of the upper abdomen with subsequent 1.25- and 5-
mm collimation axial images reviewed in conjunction with coronal and sagittal
reformats.

FINDINGS:

The evaluation of the mediastinum demonstrates several mediastinal lymph nodes
not pathologically enlarged, with extensive calcification of the subcarinal
and paraesophageal lymph nodes, unchanged since the prior study.  No hilar
lymphadenopathy is demonstrated.  The aorta and pulmonary arteries are
unremarkable within the limitation of this unenhanced study.  The heart size
is mildly enlarged.  There is no pericardial or pleural effusion.

The imaged portion of the upper abdomen demonstrates calcified gallstones.
Hypodense liver lesion is noted, series 3, image 42, 2 cm in size,
representing cyst.  The spleen is unremarkable.  The kidneys are within normal
limits.  The adrenals and the pancreas do not demonstrate any focal
abnormality.

There are no bone lesions worrisome for malignancy.

The airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally.  The evaluation
of the lungs demonstrates unchanged small cyst in the left apex, series 5,
image 47.  Several calcified granuloma are unchanged.  The noncalcified
pulmonary nodules are stable as well.  Focal areas of atelectasis in right
middle lobe and lingula are unchanged as well.  Focal area of ground glass in
the left lower lobe may represent minimal area of atelectasis or focal
infection.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Stable pulmonary nodules with evidence of granulomatous exposure.  In the
absence of risk factors, no further followup is indicated.
2.  Calcified gallstone.
3.  Liver cyst.
4.  Evidence of prior granulomatous exposure.
5.  Nodular scarring in lingula and right middle lobe, unchanged.

radiology_facts.report_text:
HISTORY:  Rectal cancer, concern for metastatic disease.

TECHNIQUE:  Helical acquisition through the chest, abdomen and pelvis was
performed following the administration of intravenous Optiray 350 contrast
material and oral contrast material.  Acquisition through the kidneys was also
performed in delayed fashion.  Comparison was made to prior chest CT from
___ and ___.

Comparison is made to prior MR from ___, and also to multiple prior
abdominal CT examinations.

FINDINGS:
CHEST:  There is a stable punctate calcification along the posterior left
pleura (3:14), likely a tiny granuloma.  There is a stable 4-mm right upper
lobe nodule (2:21), unchanged from ___.  There is a small region of
linear atelectasis or scarring adjacent to the fissure within the superior
segment of the right lower lobe (3:24).  There is a stable small region of
atelectasis or scarring at the anterior right middle lobe (3:35).  There is a
tiny calcified granuloma at the left lung base (3:53).  Calcified mediastinal
lymph nodes are again seen.  The heart is within normal limits.  There is a
tiny right thyroid nodule, 4 mm (3:4).  The osseous structures are intact.

ABDOMEN:  There is a stable hepatic cyst.  There is stable heterogeneity
within segment VIII, stable compared to recent prior exams and decreased
compared to more remote prior exams, corresponding to reported site of
previous hepatic abscesses.  There are two tiny hypodense pancreatic lesions,
measuring 3 mm at the anterior body (3:52), and 3 mm at the pancreatic tail
(3:57).  Cholelithiasis is present.  The solid and hollow abdominal organs are
otherwise within normal limits.  No lymphadenopathy is seen.  The osseous
structures are intact.

PELVIS:  Prominent presacral lymph nodes are again noted, as seen on prior MR,
measuring 8 mm and 7 mm on this study (3:95).  The largest lymph node is again
seen on the right in the perirectal region, measuring 9 mm (3:104).  There is
limited visualization of the patient's known rectal tumor, which is better
seen on MR.  ___ remaining pelvic viscera are within normal limits.  The
osseous structures are intact.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Limited visualization of known rectal tumor, better seen on MR, with
stable perirectal and presacral lymphadenopathy.  Stable 4-mm right upper lobe
pulmonary nodule, for which continued followup with CT is advised. The nodule
is stable from ___.
2.  Stable region of heterogeneity within the anterolateral aspect of hepatic
segment VIII, reportedly corresponding to the site of prior hepatic abscesses.
This is not significantly changed compared to recent studies.
3.  Two small 3-mm pancreatic hypodensities, for which further evaluation with
MRCP is recommended.
4.  Cholelithiasis.
5.  Tiny 4-mm right thyroid nodule.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/O CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ year old man who presented in shock and respiratory failure w/
presumed ingestion, EGD w/ severe esophagitis, c/f mediastinal involvement//
assess for mediastinal involvement from caustic ingestion, EGD w/ severe
esophagitis

TECHNIQUE:  Noncontrast CT of the chest with multiplanar reformats

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 8.8 s, 33.9 cm; CTDIvol = 8.9 mGy (Body) DLP = 288.8
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 301 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  No prior chest CT for comparison

FINDINGS: 

NECK, THORACIC INLET, AXILLAE, CHEST WALL: Few tiny nodules seen within the
thyroid gland.  There is no significant supraclavicular or axillary
lymphadenopathy. There is a small soft tissue nodule within the subcutaneous
tissues of the left lower lateral chest wall measuring 1.5 x 1.0 cm (series 5,
image 182).

UPPER ABDOMEN: NG tube is seen folded in the gastric body with its tip
projected in the fundus, this should be repositioned.

MEDIASTINUM: Noncontrast appearance of the heart and great vessels is within
normal limits.  Calcific atherosclerotic changes seen involving the coronary
arteries.  There is no significant mediastinal lymphadenopathy.  There is no
pericardial effusion.  The esophagus shows NG tube in situ.  No mediastinal
fluid collection.  No evidence of pneumomediastinum or mediastinal stranding.

HILA: Limited assessment of the hila on this noncontrast study, no gross hilar
lymphadenopathy.

PLEURA: No pleural effusions or pneumothorax.
LUNG:

1. PARENCHYMA: Few small nodules are seen in the both lungs for example 4 mm
nodule in the anterior segment of the right upper lobe open (series 4 image
30), 4 mm nodule in the inferior lingula (series 4, image 40), likely
nonspecific.  No suspicious pulmonary nodule or mass identified.  Few areas of
scattered ground-glass density seen in the right lower lobe.  Subsegmental
atelectasis involving the medial segment of the right middle lobe.  Mild
dependent atelectasis involving the left lower lobe.
2. AIRWAYS:  Endotracheal tube seen with its tip 2 cm above the carina.
CHEST CAGE: No suspicious bony abnormality.  Degenerative changes are noted in
the thoracic spine.

IMPRESSION: 

1. No significant mediastinal abnormality.  The NG tube seen coiled in the
gastric body with tip pointing up to the gastric fundus, this should be
repositioned.

2. Few less than 4 mm pulmonary nodules, are likely nonspecific.  No
suspicious parenchymal lung abnormality.

3. Incidental small soft tissue nodule in the left lateral chest wall, this
should be assessed on clinical exam.

radiology_facts.report_text:
PROCEDURE:  CT chest with contrast on ___.

COMPARISON:  CT torso ___.

TECHNIQUE:  After the administration of 75 cc of IV Optiray, contiguous axial
images were obtained from the thoracic inlet to the subdiaphragmatic area.
Thinner slice 5-mm and 1.25-mm images were reconstructed in the axial plane at
different window algorithm.  Sagittal/coronal reformatted images were also
obtained for evaluation.

HISTORY:  ___ man with metastatic melanoma, evaluate disease status
and include bi-dimensional measurements for all lesions and create an oncology
table.

FINDINGS:  There are multiple mediastinal lymph nodes.  The lymph nodes
located in the aortopulmonary window and in the prevascular location are all
less than 1 cm in size and not changed since the prior examination.  The
largest of the mediastinal lymph nodes located in the right hilar and
subcarinal area has substantially increased in size on today's examination.
The right subcarinal lymph node measures 4 x 5.5 cm while the right hilar
lymph node measures 2.7 x 3 cm.  The subcarinal lymph node is designated as
target lesion #1.  The right infrahilar soft tissue mass measures 4.8 x 3.9 cm
and is slightly enlarged from the previous examination(target#2
); however, the post- obstructive bronchiectasis that were filled with mucoid
impaction has partially resolved.
On today's examination, the superior segment of the right lower lobe is re-
aerated with slight reexpansion of the right lower lung.  No lung lesion
suspicious for metastasis is noted in either lung.  No pleural or pericardial
effusion is noted.
The heart size is within normal limits.
The bony structures do not show any lesion suspicious for malignancy and/or
infection. The limited evaluation of the abdomen on this contrast-enhanced
examination demonstrate multiple hypodense lesions with probable enhancing rim
seen in the liver, the largest lesion is located in the right posterior
portion of the liver.
There is also increase in size of the bilateral adrenal nodules; the right
adrenal nodule measures 1.1 x 1.2 cm while the left adrenal nodule measures
2.9 x 3 cm.

The right thyroid nodule is smaller in size on today's examination.

IMPRESSION:

1.  Interval progression of metastatic disease in the chest and lung
characterized by increase in size of the right hilar, subcarinal lymph node
and right infrahilar soft tissue mass; however, with a less post-obstructive
mucoid impaction.

2.  New liver lesions and increase in size of the bilateral adrenal metastatic
lesions.


The new oncology table has been placed online.

radiology_facts.report_text:
CT TORSO

COMPARISON:  None.

HISTORY:  Metastatic melanoma.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axial acquired images of the chest, abdomen and pelvis were
obtained.  130 cc of Optiray IV contrast was administered.  Coronal and
sagittal reformats were performed.

FINDINGS:  
CT CHEST:  There is a 2.0 x 1.8 cm right thyroid gland nodule.  Scattered
subcentimeter prevascular and pretracheal lymph nodes are identified.  A large
subcarinal lymph node measuring 3.1 x 4.3 cm (103, 32) is designated as target
lesion  #1.  Multiple right hilar lymph nodes (103, 30) measure up to 2.5 cm x
1.7 cm.  A large lower right hilar soft tissue mass (103, 39) measures
approximately 3.5 cm x 3.6 cm is designated as target lesion #2, and appears
to obstruct the right lower lobe basal trunk bronchus. Subsequently, extensive
mucus impaction is identified in the right lower lobe basilar segments.  The
soft tissue mass appears to cause mass effect and narrowing of the right
middle lobe bronchus.  Secretions are seen within the distal trachea, proximal
left main stem bronchus and right main bronchus. There is no left hilar
adenopathy.  The left main stem bronchus and its branches are patent to the
subsegmental level.  Areas of emphysematous changes are identified primarily
in the right upper lobe.  There are no other lung masses or nodules
identified.  There is no pericardial or pleural effusion identified.
Calcifications of the left anterior descending artery are noted. The great
vessels are unremarkable.

CT OF THE ABDOMEN:  The spleen, pancreas, kidneys, liver, and gallbladder are
unremarkable.  A 2.1 x 2.0 cm lesion is identified in the tail of the left
adrenal gland, which enhances upon contrast administration and is designated
as target lesion  #3. This lesion is concerning for adrenal metastases.  The
right adrenal gland is normal.  Within a focal segment of jejunal bowel loops
(103, 88) there is a large filling defect concerning for a jejunal mass.
Adjacent to surgical sutures seen within the small bowel, there is a small
cystic-appearing lesion (103, 96), which likely represents a peritoneal
inclusion cyst secondary to post-surgical changes.  There is no free fluid or
free air.  There is no mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy
identified.

CT OF THE PELVIS:  The prostate gland, bladder, sigmoid colon and rectum are
unremarkable.  There is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy identified.  

BONE WINDOWS:  An old healed right inferior pubic ramus fracture is
identified.  There are no suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions identified.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Right lower hilar soft tissue mass obstructing the right lower lobe
bronchus trunk to basilar segments causing extensive mucus impaction.  
2.  Subcarinal and right hilar pathologically enlarged lymph nodes.
3.  Right thyroid nodule.  Ultrasound is recommended for further evaluation.
4.  Left adrenal nodule measuring 2.1 x 2.0 cm, concerning for metastases.  
5.  Focal small bowel filling defect concerning for small bowel mass as
described above.

An oncology table was created under Care Web with bidimensional measurements.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with blood per rectum, cancer on colonoscopy//
New diagnosis of colorectal cancer via colonoscopy. Eval for mets.

TECHNIQUE:  Multi detector CT of the chest was performed after the
administration of intravenous contrast.  Axial coronal and sagittal
reconstructions were acquired.  Maximum intensity projections were also
acquired

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:

mGy-cm.

radiology_facts.report_text:
CT CHEST WITHOUT CONTRAST

REASON FOR EXAM:  ___ male patient with abnormal chest x-ray showing
bilateral hilar and right paratracheal adenopathy suggestive of sarcoidosis.
Please evaluate for adenopathy.

TECHNIQUE:  Chest MDCT was performed without contrast using 5-mm and 1.25-mm
axial slice thickness.  Supine expiration and prone images were also obtained.
Coronal reformations were reviewed in expiration and inspiration.

FINDINGS:  Multiple bihilar and mediastinal enlarged lymph nodes, up to 13 mm
in the right paratracheal region, 19 x 31 mm in the subcarinal region, 10 mm
in the left paratracheal region, 7 mm in the paraesophageal region and 11 mm 
in the aortopulmonary window.

Multiple lung nodules are scattered throughout the lungs, mostly in a
perilymphatic distribution.  Clustered peribronchovascular nodules are in the
right lower lobe. Lung nodules are almost all sub-4-mm wide, except one
11- mm nodule in the left upper lobe at the bifurcation of lingular segments
(104:128). Airways are patent to the subsegmental level. There is no pleural
or pericardial effusion.  Heart size is normal.  The main pulmonary artery is
up to 3.2 cm. Gynecomastia is asymmetrical, more marked on the left. The
thymus is normal for age.

Bones are normal.  Altough this study was not tailored for subdiaphragmatic
evaluation, the upper abdomen is unremarkable.  The spleen is normal.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Bihilar and mediastinal lymphadenopathy associated with multiple sub-4mm
mostly perilymphatic lung nodules, consistent with sarcoidosis.
2.  Left upper lobe peribronchovascular 11-mm lung nodule, probably related to
sarcoidosis, but should be closely followed up in three months to assess
stability.
3.  Enlarged pulmonary artery, could be due to pulmonary hypertension.
4.  Asymmetric gynecomastia, more marked on the left.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/O CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ with trauma. S/p fall. Not on A/C. Chest pain.  // fractures,
hematoma other signs of trauma?

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector helical scanning of the chest was performed and
reconstructed as contiguous 5- and 1.25-mm thick axial, 2.5-mm thick coronal
and parasagittal, and 8 x 8 mm MIPs axial images.

DOSE:  DLP: 654 mGy-cm

COMPARISON:  ___ CTA chest

FINDINGS: 

Two right-sided pulmonary nodules are again identified, specifically a 4 mm
upper lobe pulmonary nodule (4; 134) and a 4 mm right lower lobe pulmonary
nodule (4; 139).  Smaller subpleural right lower lobe nodule is unchanged as
well (4; 170). There is no new pulmonary nodule. The lungs are clear without
focal consolidation, effusion, or pneumothorax. The central airways are
patent. There is no mediastinal or axillary adenopathy.

The unenhanced heart and great vessels are grossly unremarkable.

Included portion of the upper abdomen is unremarkable.

Degenerative changes are noted at the left glenohumeral joint. Compression
deformity of T7 with endplate sclerosis is unchanged noting and vacuum disc
phenomenon at T7-T8. There is no acute fracture.

Postoperative changes of left hemithyroidectomy are again noted as detailed on
CT of cervical spine performed the same day.

IMPRESSION: 

No acute intrathoracic process.  No evidence of acute fracture.
Right-sided pulmonary nodules up to 4 mm in size for which followup exam in ___
year from prior study can be performed if patient has risk factors such as
smoking or malignancy, otherwise, no additional imaging is necessary.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CTA CHEST WITH CONTRAST

INDICATION:  History: ___ with chest pain, dyspnea// Pulmonary Embolism

TECHNIQUE:  Axial multidetector CT images were obtained through the thorax
after the uneventful administration of intravenous contrast.  Reformatted
coronal, sagittal, thin slice axial images, and oblique maximal intensity
projection images were submitted to PACS and reviewed.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 2.2 s, 29.2 cm; CTDIvol = 15.2 mGy (Body) DLP = 442.7
mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 0.6 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 3.3 mGy (Body) DLP = 1.7
mGy-cm.
   3) Stationary Acquisition 1.8 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 9.9 mGy (Body) DLP = 5.0
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 449 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  CT chest ___

FINDINGS: 

HEART AND VASCULATURE: Pulmonary vasculature is adequately opacified through
the segmental level without filling defect to indicate a pulmonary embolus. 
The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber without evidence of dissection or
intramural hematoma.  There is mild cardiomegaly, similar to prior.  No
pericardial effusion is seen.

AXILLA, HILA, AND MEDIASTINUM: No axillary, mediastinal, or hilar
lymphadenopathy is present.  No mediastinal mass.

PLEURAL SPACES: No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

LUNGS/AIRWAYS: There is bandlike subsegmental atelectasis in the right lower
lobe, right middle lobe and lingula.  There is no consolidation concerning for
infection.  4 mm pulmonary nodules within the right middle lobe (301:108) and
right lower lobe (301:125) are stable since ___.  Central airways are patent.

BASE OF NECK: The patient is status post left hemithyroidectomy.  Visualized
portions of the base of the neck are otherwise unremarkable.

ABDOMEN: There is a small hiatal hernia.  The included portion of the upper
abdomen is otherwise unremarkable.

BONES: Moderate compression deformity of the T7 vertebra with endplate
sclerosis is unchanged.  No suspicious osseous abnormality is seen.? There is
no acute fracture.


IMPRESSION:


1. No evidence of pulmonary embolism or aortic abnormality.  No acute
pulmonary parenchymal findings.  Subsegmental atelectasis in the bilateral
lung bases.
2. Moderate compression deformity of the T7 vertebra, unchanged.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CTA CHEST WANDW/O CANDRECONS, NON-CORONARY

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with new onset SOB and multiple risks: infection,
Mutliple myeloma and drug: Revlimid.  // R/o PE

TECHNIQUE:  Multi detector CT images were obtained through the chest in
arterial phase after administration of IV Omnipaque contrast.  Axial images
were interpreted in conjunction with coronal, sagittal, right oblique MIP and
left oblique MIP reformats.

212.60 mGy-cm

COMPARISON:  Comparison is made with CT abdomen and pelvis dated ___.

FINDINGS: 

CHEST CTA:

The thoracic aorta is normal caliber without evidence of aneurysm or
dissection.  The main, lobar, and segmental pulmonary arteries are well
opacified without filling defect.  Within a subsegmental branch of the
pulmonary arterial system within the posterior left lower lobe, there appears
to be intraluminal filling defect (6:212) which may represent a tiny pulmonary
embolism versus contrast mixing. The remainder of the great vessels have a
normal appearance.

CHEST:

The thyroid is normal. Axillary, supraclavicular, mediastinal, and hilar lymph
nodes are not pathologically enlarged.  The heart and mediastinum are normal.
The pericardium is intact without effusion.  Airways are patent to the
subsegmental levels.

Lung windows demonstrate bibasilar dependent atelectasis. Additionally, there
is a 1.2 cm left lower lobe, pleural-based nodule abutting the superior margin
of the left hemidiaphragm (6:231). There is no evidence of pleural effusion or
pneumothorax.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:  No focal lytic or sclerotic lesion concerning for
malignancy.

The esophagus is within normal limits. Although this study is not tailored for
the examination of subdiaphragmatic contents, the imaged portions of the
intra-abdominal organs are grossly unremarkable.

IMPRESSION: 

1. Tiny, subsegmental, left lower lobe pulmonary embolism. No evidence of
central, lobar, or segmental PE.

2. 1.2 cm pleural-based pulmonary nodule within the left lower lobe, not well
visualized on the prior examination.  Limited chest CT over the left lower
lobe could be performed 3 months for further evaluation.

NOTIFICATION:  Findings were entered into the radiology critical results
dashboard by Dr. ___ at 3:14pm on ___, 1 minute after interpretation.

radiology_facts.report_text:
CT TORSO

INDICATION:  History of lymphadenopathy, breast CA with lumpectomy in ___. 
Assess for lymphadenopathy.

COMPARISON:  No prior CT imaging in this institution for comparison.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axially acquired images from the lung apices to the pubic
symphysis displayed with 5-mm slice thickness with oral and IV contrast. 
Coronal and sagittal reformats have been provided.

FINDINGS:

CT THORAX:
There is an enlarged left supraclavicular lymph node measuring 1.7x3.2cm (
3;17).
There is bulky left axillary adenopathy, the largest measuring 2.1 x 1.3 cm
(series 3, image 19).  Surgical clips are noted in the right axilla.  No
breast mass identified.  
No further mediastinal, hilar or internal mammary adenopathy.  
There is a left paravertebral mass identified in the lower thoracic spine
measuring 1 cm in maximum thickness and extending over a craniocaudal length
of 7.6 cm (series 5, image 27).  
There is a large left pleural effusion with compressive atelectasis of the
left lower lobe.  
No pulmonary masses or nodules.  
No pericardial effusion.

CT ABDOMEN:
There is a large retrocrural lymph node measuring 1.5 x 1.4 cm (series 3,
image 46).  
There is a large retroperitoneal conglomerate lymph node mass extending all
the way to the bifurcation with soft tissue encasement of the aorta and IVC.  
The largest nodes are in the aortocaval region measuring 2.6 x 2.6 cm (series
3, image 58) and paraaortic region on the left side, inferior to the left
renal vein measuring 1.7 x 2.9 cm (series 3, image 62).  No further mesenteric
adenopathy identified.  
There is a segment VII low-density lesion too small to characterize (series 3,
image 49). No intra- or extra-hepatic biliary dilatation.  The portal vein is
patent.  
There is splenomegaly of 13 cm (series 5, image 21) with an abnormal
heterogenous attenuation concerning for lymphomatous infiltration. 
Adrenal glands and pancreas are unremarkable.  
There are small bilateral renal cysts.  
Small trace perihepatic and perisplenic ascites is noted.


CT PELVIS:
The retroperitoneal conglomerate mass extends from the level of bifurcation
along the external iliac chains bilaterally to the level of the common femoral
vessels. Again there is soft tissue encasement of the common iliac and
external iliac vessels. The conglomerate mass measures 2.2 x 5.9 cm on the
right side at level of external iliac vessels(series 3, image 89) and 6.1 x
2.3 cm on the left side.  
There is also bulky bilateral inguinal adenopathy, the largest on the right
measuring 1.5 x 2.3cm and on the left side 1.7 x 1.4 cm.  
The bladder, rectum, sigmoid colon are unremarkable.  Small trace of free
fluid is noted within the pelvis.  
No adnexal masses.


CT OSSEOUS SKELETON:  
There is an S-shaped scoliosis of the thoracolumbar spine with multilevel
degenerative change.  
Internal fixation rods are identified in the lower three lumbar vertebrae.


IMPRESSION:  
1. Extensive axillary, supraclavicular and inguinal adenopathy. 
2. There is left paravertebral and retroperitineal conglomerate lymph node
mass.
3. Large left pleural effusion with compressive atelectasis of the left lower
lobe.  
4. Heterogenous attenuation to the spleen concerning for lymphomatous
infiltration.

All of the above findings are concerning for lymphoma. Less likely
differential would include metastatic disease.  Findings posted on the
critical dashboard.

radiology_facts.report_text:
CT TORSO

INDICATION:  Lymphoma followup.

COMPARISON:  CT ___.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axially acquired images from the lung apices to pubic
symphysis displayed with 5 mm slice thickness with oral and IV contrast. 
Coronal and sagittal reformations have been provided.

FINDINGS:

CT THORAX:
Significant decrease in size of the left supraclavicular and axillary lymph
nodes with no new mediastinal, internal mammary or hilar adenopathy.  
There is decrease in size but residual left pleural effusion with compressive
atelectasis of the left lower lobe again noted.  
There is also decrease in the amount of soft tissue thickening in the left
paravertebral region of the lumbar spine now measuring 3.0 x 5.6 cm (series
301B, image 27) previously 1.0 x 7.6 cm craniocaudal.
4 mm nodule identified in the posterior aspect of the right lower lobe within
an area of gravitational atelectasis (series 2, image 32).  This was not
visualized on prior CT.  However it is likely reactive.  No further lung
masses or nodules identified.


CT ABDOMEN:  
Significant decrease in size in the right retrocrural node, conglomerate lymph
node mass in the retroperitoneum and soft tissue encasement of the aorta and
IVC since prior study.  Two distinct areas of residual adenopathy are seen:
the first in the aortocaval region measuring 0.8 x 1.3 cm (series 2, image 57)
and measuring  7x6mm in the left para-aortic region at the level of the aortic
bifurcation, ( Se 2:70) -  again both are considerably decreased in size since
prior CT. 
Stable low-density lesion identified within segment VII of the liver (series
2, image 2424) stable and unchanged when compared to prior imaging.  No new
liver lesion. Stable post -cholecystectomy intra-and hepatic biliary
dilatation.  
Spleen has also decreased in size since prior imaging and now measures 7.7 cm
(previously 9.5 cm series 2, image 50) and appears more homogenous.  
Both adrenal glands and pancreas are unremarkable.  
Both kidneys are normal apart from a simple cyst in the interpolar region of
the right kidney (series 2, image 54) stable and unchanged when compared to
prior imaging.  

CT PELVIS:
Again significant decrease in the amount of retroperitoneal conglomerate
adenopathy extending along the right common and external iliac veins
bilaterally.  This is now almost unmeasurable apart from some mild stranding
around the vessels. 
Small trace of free fluid is noted within the pelvis.  
Rectum, sigmoid colon, bladder and both adnexa are unremarkable.  
Dystrophic calcification seen in a possibly atrophic uterus, stable. 
There is also significant decrease in size in the inguinal adenopathy, the
largest node now measuring 5 x 12 mm in the right inguinal region previously
measuring 1.5 x 2.3 cm (series 2, image 94).

CT OSSEOUS SKELETON:  
S-shaped scoliosis of the thoracolumbar spine with multilevel degenerative
change noted. There is an internal fixation device noted in the lower three
lumbar vertebrae.  
No new osseous destructive lesion.


IMPRESSION:  
1. Extensive reduction in size of the previously described axillary,
retroperitoneal and inguinal adenopathy since prior imaging consistent with
disease response. 

2. Spleen now appears homogenous and has also decreased in size. 

3. Residual but smaller left pleural effusion with compressive atelectasis of
the left lower lobe.

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  Patient with history of right breast cancer treated with
lumpectomy and radiation.  Patient now with lymphoma, restaging.

COMPARISON:  Comparison is made to CT torso performed ___ and
___.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axial contrast-enhanced images were obtained from the
thoracic inlet to the pelvic outlet.  Coronal and sagittal reformations were
performed.

FINDINGS:  There is a stable 6.8 mm hypodensity in the right thyroid lobe,
unchanged from ___ (3:10).  There is no supraclavicular, axillary,
mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy present.  Airways are patent to the
subsegmental level.  Heart size is normal without evidence of pericardial
effusion.  The great vessels appear unremarkable.  Atherosclerotic
calcifications are noted throughout the thoracic aorta without associated
aneurysmal change.  Posterior to the right breast, there is focal right upper
lobe pleural thickening with minimal associated intraseptal thickening, likely
reflecting post-radiation change (3:39).  In the superior right upper lobe
there is a stable 4.6 mm pulmonary nodule (3:26), unchanged from ___.  Minimal atelectactic changes are seen in the dependent portions of the
lung and surrounding the descending aorta.  There is a decrease in the left-
sided pleural effusion, now small in size.

CT ABDOMEN:  In hepatic segment VII, there is a stable 5 mm hypodensity likely
representing a simple hepatic cyst.  No other focal lesions are identified
within the liver.  There is no intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation.  The
gallbladder has been surgically removed.  The common bile duct is slightly
prominent, likely reflecting post-cholecystectomy change.  The pancreas is
unremarkable.  The bilateral adrenal glands are of normal limb thickness
without convex margin to suggest mass.  Bilateral kidneys are normal in size.
There is a stable simple renal cyst in the right interpolar region.  No
hydroureter seen.  The stomach, small and large bowel within the abdomen are
unremarkable.  There is no retroperitoneal, mesenteric or portacaval
lymphadenopathy identified.

CT PELVIS:  The appendix is not visualized, though there are no secondary
signs to suggest inflammation.  There is no free fluid within the pelvis.  The
rectum is unremarkable.  No significant pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy is
demonstrated.  The bladder is compressed and not fully evaluated.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:  Redemonstration of S-shaped thoracolumbar scoliosis with
multilevel degenerative change including stable Grade 1 anterolisthesis of L4
on L5.  Internal fixation devices are noted throughout the lower lumbar
vertebrae without evidence of hardware failure.  No suspicious lytic or
blastic lesions are identified.

IMPRESSION:
1.  No lymphadenopathy identified on current study, representing a decrease
compared to prior study and overall consistent with continued disease
response.
2.  Stable right thyroid nodule.
3.  Decreased left pleural effusion, now small in size.
4.  Right upper lobe pleural thickening, likely reflecting post-radiation
change.
5.  Stable subcentimeter right pulmonary nodule.

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  Evaluate for changes in disease status in a patient with
metastatic renal cell carcinoma on Sutent therapy after cycle #2.

COMPARISON:  MRI of the thoracic spine from ___ and CT of the
torso from ___.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT-acquired axial images of chest, abdomen and pelvis were
obtained after administration of 100 cc Omnipaque intravenous contrast
material.  Enteric contrast also given.  Three-minute delay abdomen images
obtained.  Coronal and sagittal reformats provided and reviewed.

DLP:  1803.92 mGy-cm.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:  The visualized portion of the thyroid gland appears normal.  There is
no supraclavicular, axillary, mediastinal, or hilar lymphadenopathy.  Thoracic
aorta is normal in caliber, as is the pulmonary artery.  There are
atherosclerotic calcifications in the aortic arch but the arch vessels are
patent.  The heart size is normal and there is no pericardial effusion.  The
tracheobronchial tree is normal.  There are no concerning pulmonary nodules. 
There are small focal areas of ground glass and nodular opacity in the right
lung (2:21, 24).  These findings are new.  There is a 2 mm pulmonary nodule in
the left lower lobe which is smaller than on the prior examination (2:25). 
There is no pneumothorax or pleural effusion.

ABDOMEN:  There is a small hiatal hernia.  The liver enhances homogeneously. 
There is a vague subcentimeter hypodensity in the right lobe of the liver
which was present on the prior examination and is too small to characterize by
CT (2:40).  There is no bile duct dilation.  The patient is status post
cholecystectomy.  The pancreas, spleen, and adrenal glands appear normal.  A
1.8 cm simple cyst is seen in the upper pole of the right kidney.  The right
kidney is otherwise normal.  There is a 3.1 x 2.8 cm hypoenhancing mass in the
left mid kidney which extends from the renal medulla to the cortex.  There is
at least one location in which there is no cortical tissue between the mass
and the surrounding perirenal fat and there appears to be some bulging of the
capsule at this location.  Invasion to the perirenal space cannot be excluded
(2:64).  A 1.3 cm simple cyst is seen in the anterior upper pole of the left
kidney, unchanged (2:61).

The stomach, small bowel, and large bowel appear normal aside from colonic
diverticulosis without evidence of diverticulitis and an anterior right
abdominal wall hernia into which normal appearing transverse colon has
herniated.  This hernia has progressed from the prior examination when it
merely contained fat.

The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber with patent main branches and minimal
atherosclerotic calcifications.  The portal vein is patent.  There is a
cluster of enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes near the origin of the left
renal artery measuring 1.9 x 1.1 cm, approximately the same size as on the
prior examination (2:61).  Further inferiorly, there is an enlarged 1.8 x 1.1
cm left paraaortic lymph node which is again stable in size (2:65).  There is
no new retroperitoneal or intraperitoneal lymphadenopathy.  There is no
ascites or free air.

PELVIS:  The rectum, prostate, seminal vesicles, and bladder appear normal. 
There is no pelvic mass, lymphadenopathy, or free fluid.

MUSCULOSKELETAL:  Again noted are lytic metastatic lesions in the T2 and L2
vertebral bodies which have caused pathologic compression fractures, not
significantly progressed from the prior examination (602b:45, 44).  Vague
sclerotic lesions can be seen in T7 (602b:44) and L1 (602b:47).  These are
better evaluated on the MRI.  There are severe degenerative changes of L2, L3
and L4 with another possible sclerotic lesion in L3 (602b:42).  The 1.4 x 1.4
cm sclerotic lesion in the right aspect of the sacrum is not significantly
changed from the prior examination.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Stable appearance of the primary left renal tumor which extends to the
capsular surface and where invasion into the perirenal space cannot be fully
excluded.
2.  Stable appearance of two pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the left
retroperitoneal space.
3.  New areas of focal ground-glass and nodular opacity in the right lung has
the appearance of a developing infectious or inflammatory process.
4.  A 2 mm pulmonary nodule in the left lung is smaller than on the prior
study.
5.  Multiple stable skeletal metastases as outlined above, no new lesions
identified.
6.  Anterior abdominal wall hernia through which transverse colon has now
herniated.

radiology_facts.report_text:
CHEST CT, ___

HISTORY:  Renal cell carcinoma, on therapy.

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector helical scanning of the chest was coordinated with
imaging of the abdomen and pelvis to be reported separately on intravenous
infusion of 130 cc Omnipaque nonionic iodinated contrast agent reconstructed
as contiguous 5- and 1.25-mm thick axial, 5 mm thick coronal and parasagittal,
and 8 x 8 mm MIPs images, compared to CT imaging of the chest since at least
___, most recently ___.

FINDINGS:

Small region of bronchiolar nodulation in the right upper lobe present on
___ has resolved.  Right lung is otherwise clear.

Punctate lesion, left lower lobe, 2:25, cited on the previous study has not
changed in the past two months.  I am not sure if it was actually larger on
___, when there was a fair amount of motion at the level of
imaging, but it is possible that it is a shrinking solitary metastasis.  Aside
from mild linear atelectasis at the base, left lung is otherwise clear. 
Asbestos-related pleural plaque, predominates in the left hemithorax, 4:90. 
It is accompanied by very mild smooth thickening of the left costal pleural
surface, for example 4:108, which was present on ___, 2:34.  Left
hemithorax is slightly smaller than the right, also unchanged.  There is no
discrete pleural mass or effusion to suggest pleural malignancy, and there are
no findings of asbestosis in the lungs.

As before, contrast is retained in the esophagus, with no focal lesion. This
study is not designed for subdiaphragmatic diagnosis, which will be rendered
in a separate report.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Solitary tiny left lung nodule, unchanged since ___ could
conceivably be an isolated involuted pulmonary metastasis.  There are no new
lung lesions, adenopathy or any other evidence of active intrathoracic
malignancy.  Previous minimal bronchiolitis in the right lung has resolved.
2.  Asbestos-related pleural plaques, no evidence of pleural malignancy, lung
cancer, or asbestosis.

radiology_facts.report_text:
HISTORY:  Mass seen at ___ a few months ago.  Presenting with pericardial
effusion. ?Lung mass.  

COMPARISON:  Multiple previous chest radiographs, most recent dated ___. 

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector CT of the thorax was performed without contrast. 
Coronal and sagittal reformats were provided for interpretation.  

FINDINGS:

There are two nodules within the right middle lobe measuring 6 mm (5:181) and
4 mm (5:188) respectively.  There are also pleural-based nodules within the
right middle and lower lobes measuring 4 mm (5:175) and 6 mm (5:166)
respectively.  There are moderate-sized bilateral pleural effusions.  There is
complete atelectasis of the left lower lobe with partial atelectasis of the
right lower lobe, likely secondary to compression from the effusions.  

There is a small pericardial effusion.  A single-chamber pacemaker is noted
with its tip in the right ventricle.  There is mild cardiomegaly.  Multiple
subcentimeter mediastinal lymph nodes are noted and are likely reactive.  No
axillary adenopathy. The thyroid gland is unremarkable.  

The visualized upper abdominal viscera is unremarkable.  Multilevel
degenerative change is noted within the lower thoracic and upper lumbar spine.
Osseous structures are otherwise unremarkable.  

IMPRESSION:

1.  Multiple subcentimeter nodules as described within the right middle and
lower lobes, with the largest measuring 6 mm.  Correlation with the previous
imaging would be of benefit to ensure stability.  Follow-up CT in ___ months
is recommended as per ___ society recommendations.  

2.  Moderate-sized bilateral pleural effusions. 

3.  Small pericardial effusion.

radiology_facts.report_text:
CHEST CT, ___

HISTORY:  Rib pain.  Possible mediastinal mass.

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector helical scanning of the chest was coordinated with
intravenous infusion of Optiray 350 nonionic iodinated contrast agent
reconstructed as contiguous 5- and 1.25-mm thick axial and 5-mm thick coronal
and paramedian sagittal images, read in conjunction with chest radiographs
from ___ and ___.

FINDINGS:  

Irregular peribronchial soft tissue infiltration in the right upper lobe
extends upward from the upper pole of the right hilus into the apical segment,
continuous with mild, permeatice lymph node enlargement in hilus that
marginates but does not impinge on the arch of the azygos vein.  The orifice
to the posterior segment of the right upper lobe bronchus is severely narrowed
accounting for most of the moderate degree of volume loss in the right upper
lobe responsible for anterior migration of the major fissure and slight
rightward shift of the mediastinum.  A spiculated 13 mm wide nodule in the
right upper lobe abuts the major fissure, 3:24.  Another 5.4 mm wide
spiculated nodule is close to the minor fissure, 3:28, a 7 mm wide nodule is
deeper in the right middle lobe, 3:34, a 6-mm right lower lobe nodule is
within 2 cm of the posterior costal pleural surface, 3:38, and a very dense,
almost mass-like lesion with consolidative features in the posterior segment
of the right lower lobe, 29 x 18 mm, abuts the paraspinal pleura separated by
a 4 mm wide subpleural nodule, 3:34.

Prevascular lymph nodes at the thoracic inlet range in size up to 10 mm, 2:14,
right lower paratracheal nodes are 9.5 mm wide and diaphragmatic nodes in the
right cardiophrenic sulcus, are 6 mm across.  A chain of nodes extending from
the left lower paraspinal to the retrocrural space measure up to 11 mm across,
2:53.  However the largest lymph nodes are in the abdomen including a 22 mm
wide left gastric node and a dozen periaortic retroperitoneal nodes ranging up
to 27 mm across.  This study is not designed for subdiaphragmatic evaluation
except to note a homogeneous liver and no adrenal mass although the left
adrenal is difficult to separate from adjacent adenopathy. There is no
spleen, and no vascular clips to support the presumptive explanation of
splenectomy.

IMPRESSION:  Interpretation of findings would depend upon clinical history,
for example, has the patient had splenectomy for lymphoma or CLL?  The chest
radiograph requisition from ___ cites a history of neurosarcoid.

A unifying diagnosis for severe abdominal adenopathy, less extensive
intrathoracic lymph node enlargement and multiple lung lesions including
nodules, extensive peribronchial infiltration (the right upper lobe lesion
which prompted this CT scan) and mass-like consolidation includes lymphoma and
sarcoidosis.  Any one of the lung lesions however could be a bronchogenic
carcinoma.  Of note, comparing chest radiographs it is possible that an
early stage of the large right upper lobe lesion was present on ___, but unfortunately that does not clarify the diagnosis.

Dr. ___ I discussed these findings and their clinical significance by
telelphone at the time of dictation.

sections.technique:
Multidetector helical scanning of the chest was coordinated with
intravenous infusion of Optiray 350 nonionic iodinated contrast agent
reconstructed as contiguous 5- and 1.25-mm thick axial and 5-mm thick coronal
and paramedian sagittal images, read in conjunction with chest radiographs
from ___ and ___.
sections.findings:
Irregular peribronchial soft tissue infiltration in the right upper lobe
extends upward from the upper pole of the right hilus into the apical segment,
continuous with mild, permeatice lymph node enlargement in hilus that
marginates but does not impinge on the arch of the azygos vein.  The orifice
to the posterior segment of the right upper lobe bronchus is severely narrowed
accounting for most of the moderate degree of volume loss in the right upper
lobe responsible for anterior migration of the major fissure and slight
rightward shift of the mediastinum.  A spiculated 13 mm wide nodule in the
right upper lobe abuts the major fissure, 3:24.  Another 5.4 mm wide
spiculated nodule is close to the minor fissure, 3:28, a 7 mm wide nodule is
deeper in the right middle lobe, 3:34, a 6-mm right lower lobe nodule is
within 2 cm of the posterior costal pleural surface, 3:38, and a very dense,
almost mass-like lesion with consolidative features in the posterior segment
of the right lower lobe, 29 x 18 mm, abuts the paraspinal pleura separated by
a 4 mm wide subpleural nodule, 3:34.

Prevascular lymph nodes at the thoracic inlet range in size up to 10 mm, 2:14,
right lower paratracheal nodes are 9.5 mm wide and diaphragmatic nodes in the
right cardiophrenic sulcus, are 6 mm across.  A chain of nodes extending from
the left lower paraspinal to the retrocrural space measure up to 11 mm across,
2:53.  However the largest lymph nodes are in the abdomen including a 22 mm
wide left gastric node and a dozen periaortic retroperitoneal nodes ranging up
to 27 mm across.  This study is not designed for subdiaphragmatic evaluation
except to note a homogeneous liver and no adrenal mass although the left
adrenal is difficult to separate from adjacent adenopathy. There is no
spleen, and no vascular clips to support the presumptive explanation of
splenectomy.
sections.impression:
Interpretation of findings would depend upon clinical history,
for example, has the patient had splenectomy for lymphoma or CLL?  The chest
radiograph requisition from ___ cites a history of neurosarcoid.

A unifying diagnosis for severe abdominal adenopathy, less extensive
intrathoracic lymph node enlargement and multiple lung lesions including
nodules, extensive peribronchial infiltration (the right upper lobe lesion
which prompted this CT scan) and mass-like consolidation includes lymphoma and
sarcoidosis.  Any one of the lung lesions however could be a bronchogenic
carcinoma.  Of note, comparing chest radiographs it is possible that an
early stage of the large right upper lobe lesion was present on ___, but unfortunately that does not clarify the diagnosis.

Dr. ___ I discussed these findings and their clinical significance by
telelphone at the time of dictation.

radiology_facts.report_text:
HISTORY:  ___ male with recently treated pneumonia and chronic
sarcoidosis, now with persistent cough and leukocytosis.

COMPARISON:  PET-CT ___, CT chest ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Volumetric MDCT images were acquired of the chest without contrast
on a 64 slice scanner according to department protocol.  Axial, coronal, and
sagittal reformations were produced.

FINDINGS:  Scarring and bronchiectasis within the right upper lobe is
unchanged in appearance and likely the sequela of this patients sarcoid. 
Right upper lobe chain suture reflects prior wedge resection.  Multiple new
peribronchovascular nodules are predominantly basal and peripheral, some of
which show a characteristic flame-shaped appearance (4; 113).  For example, a
right lower lobe nodule abuts the pleural surface, contains an air
bronchogram, and measures 2.8 x 2.3 cm;  a left lower lobe nodule also
contains traversing air-filled bronchi and measures 2.5 x 1.8 cm.  The pleural
surfaces are normal.  The airways remain patent and normal appearing, with a
small amount of adherent mucus within the trachea.  

Central lymph nodes are mildly enlarged from the prior study, including a
1.5-cm right lower paratracheal node, a 9-mm prevascular node, and a 1.3-cm
subcarinal node.  The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. 
The great vessels are normal in size and configuration.  A physiologic amount
of fluid is seen in the posterior superior aortic recess.  There is moderate
calcification of the visualized coronary arteries.

While this exam is not tailored for evaluation of structures beneath the
diaphragm, it shows prominent retrocrural lymph nodes.  The thyroid gland is
normal appearing.  There is moderate thoracic spinal degenerative disc change.
Compression deformity of the T12 vertebral body is similar in appearance to
the chest radiograph performed ___, but new from ___.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Marked increase in basilar and peripheral prominence of
peribronchovascular nodules, some of which have a characteristic flame-shaped
appearance suggestive of sarcoid, however, in the clinical setting of
leukocytosis and fever, infection cannot be excluded.
2.  T12 wedge deformity, unchanged from ___ and new from ___.

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  History of sarcoidosis, on immunosuppressants, now with fever and
weight loss; question infection, malignancy, sarcoid flare.

COMPARISONS:  Chest CT from ___.  Thoracic spine radiographs from
___.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axial imaging was obtained through the chest following the
administration of intravenous contrast material.  Coronal and sagittal
reformats were completed.

FINDINGS:  The thyroid gland is unremarkable.  There are no enlarged
supraclavicular or axillary lymph nodes.  Enlarged mediastinal nodes including
a 1.4 x 2.0 right paratracheal node are similar to ___.  Anterior
mediastinal nodes are slightly smaller (4:22).  1.6 x 0.9 cm left hilar node
is present.  The aorta and pulmonary artery are of normal caliber.  There are
mild coronary artery calcifications.  No pericardial effusion is noted. 
Scarring and bronchiectasis with peribronchovascular soft tissue extending to
the mediastinum in the right upper lobe is unchanged, likely sequela of
patient's sarcoidosis.  There has been significant improvement in
peribronchovascular nodules and opacities since ___.  Small scattered
opacities remain, likely sequela of sarcoidosis; for example, in the left
upper lobe (5:139).  There is no pleural effusion or pneumothorax.  The
airways are patent to the subsegmental levels.

This study is not tailored for evaluation of subdiaphragmatic structures. 
Please see dedicated CT abdomen report from the same day for further details. 


Multiple thoracic compression fractures are unchanged from the thoracic spine
radiographs from ___.  ___ bodies T6, T8, T11 and L1
demonstrate loss of more than 50% of height, as seen previously.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Overall significant improvement in pulmonary sarcoidosis with only a few
scattered opacities remaining.  Stable right upper lobe scarring and
peribronchovascular opacities, likely sequela of sarcoid.

2.  Mediastinal lymphadenopathy, improved since ___.

3.  Multiple thoracic spine wedge compression fractures, unchanged.

4.  Please see dedicated CT Abdomen report for furhter details.

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  History of sarcoidosis, on immunosuppressants, now with fever and
weight loss; question infection, malignancy, sarcoid flare.

COMPARISONS:  Chest CT from ___ and ___.  Thoracic
spine radiographs from ___.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axial imaging was obtained through the chest following the
administration of intravenous contrast material.  Coronal and sagittal
reformats were completed.

FINDINGS:  

MEDIASTINUM:  The thyroid gland is unremarkable.  There are no enlarged
supraclavicular or axillary lymph nodes.  Enlarged mediastinal nodes including
a 1.4 x 2.0 right paratracheal node are similar to ___.  Anterior
mediastinal nodes are unchanged (6:38) as well as a 13 x 10 mm left hilar
node.  The aorta and pulmonary artery are of normal caliber.  There are mild
coronary artery calcifications.  No pericardial effusion is noted.  

LUNGS AND AIRWAYS:  The airways are patent to the subsegmental levels.  There
is a new right lower lobe consolidation along the dependent portion of the
right lung and following the pleural surface curvature (representative image
7:147). Otherwise scarring and bronchiectasis with peribronchovascular soft
tissue extending to the mediastinum in the right upper lobe is unchanged,
likely sequela of the patient's sarcoidosis.  Peribronchovascular nodules and
opacities are stable from the previous exam but significantly improved from
___ as previously stated.  Small scattered opacities remain, likely
sequela of sarcoidosis; for example, in the left upper lobe (7:123).  There is
no pleural effusion or pneumothorax.  

This study is not tailored for evaluation of subdiaphragmatic structures. 
Please see dedicated CT abdomen report from the same day for further details. 


OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:  Multiple thoracic compression fractures are unchanged
from the thoracic spine radiographs from ___.  ___ bodies
T6, T8, T11 and L1 demonstrate loss of more than 50% of height, as seen
previously.

IMPRESSION:
1.  New right lower lobe opacity along the dependent portion of the lung may
represent a combination of atelectasis and infecion. Lymphoma and less likely
relapse of sarcoidosis are alternative diagnoses. Other scattered opacities
including a right upper lobe scarring and peribronchovascular opacities are
stable from the previous exam and likely sequela of sarcoid.

2.  Mediastinal lymphadenopathy, stable from prior and improved since ___.

3.  Multiple thoracic spine wedge compression fractures, unchanged.

radiology_facts.report_text:
PATIENT HISTORY:  ___ years old man with pneumatosis, portal/mesenteric venous
catheter, status post exploratory laparotomy with extended right colectomy for
ischemic colon, prior anastomosis, now status post abdominal closure,
ileostomy with ___, now with fever and tachypnea.  Assess for pneumonia.

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector helical scanning of the chest without infusion of
contrast material agent.  Images were reconstructed as contiguous 5- and
1.25-mm thick axial helical, thick coronal and parasagittal, and 8 x 8 mm MIPs
projections.

COMPARISON:  Exam is compared to chest CT of ___.

FINDINGS:  Mucoid secretions are in the lower trachea, 4:56, minimally reduced
since ___.  In the left lower lobe, there is new consolidation
involving the superior segment of the left lower lobe and mainly the
posterobasal segment of the same lobe, 4:111 to 4:163.  The bronchus for the
superior segment of the left lower lobe is obstructed by hypodense material
and might be mucus.  Adjacent to the consolidation there is an area of
ground-glass and bronchiolar nodules, 4:125.  These findings are suggestive of
pneumonia.  Right lower lobe atelectasis, 4:172, has improved since ___, now with residual ground-glass and linear opacity.  Two punctate
subpleural nodules in the right upper lobe, 4:50, 54 and 4 mm parafissural
nodule in the right lower lobe are unchanged since ___.  Severe
centrilobular emphysema and bronchial wall thickening are unchanged since the
prior examination.

Thyroid gland is unremarkable.  There is no peripheral or central
lymphadenopathy.  Great vessels have normal in size.  Heart size is moderately
enlarged.  Mild enlargement of all four chambers, but especially the left
chamber.  Aortic valve calcification are mild, 2:34, unchanged since ___.  Mild blood density is compatible with anemia.  There is no pericardial
or pleural effusion.

UPPER ABDOMEN:  Even though this exam is not tailored for abdominal imaging,
it shows normal liver, spleen, adrenals, and pancreas in a patient who has had
cholecystectomy as denoted by metal clips in the gallbladder bed.  NG tube
ends coiled in the fundus of stomach 2:39.

BONES:  There are no bone lesions suspicious for malignancy or infection.

IMPRESSION:
1. Left lower lobe pneumonia, potentialy due to aspiration.
2. Two punctate nodules in the right upper lobe and parafissural nodule in the
right lower lobe are unchanged since ___.  
3. No lymphadenopathy.  
4. Moderate cardiomegaly is unchanged since the prior examination.
